title: Yemen Reports Fourth Oil Tanker Hijacking Off Somali Coast as Piracy Surges During Iran War
summary: Yemen's Coast Guard reported the hijacking of oil tanker M/T Eureka off the Shabwa coast, the fourth such incident in recent weeks as naval forces remain diverted to the Hormuz blockade.
contents:
## Piracy Surge Off Somali Coast — M/T Eureka Hijacked

### Incident Details
Yemen's Coast Guard reported that the oil tanker M/T Eureka fell to armed hijackers off Yemen's southeastern Shabwa province. Armed assailants boarded and seized control of the vessel, steering it through the Gulf of Aden toward the Somali coast. The fate of the crew remains unknown.

### Pattern of Attacks
The hijacking marks at least the fourth attack near Somalia in recent weeks, with pirate activity escalating dramatically as an apparent consequence of the Iran war. A tanker carrying approximately 18,000 barrels of oil suffered hijacking near the Somali coast on April 21, followed by two additional vessel seizures within five days.

### Naval Diversion Factor
Officials and analysts directly link the piracy resurgence to the diversion of anti-piracy naval forces toward the Strait of Hormuz blockade and Red Sea operations. The European Union naval forces patrolling the region acknowledged that the Iran war has provided piracy groups a "window of opportunity." The UK Maritime Trade Operations raised the piracy threat level along the Somali coast to "substantial."

### Historical Context
Somalia's coastline experienced the world's worst piracy from the early to mid-2000s, with the World Bank estimating peak annual economic costs of $18 billion. An international naval coalition suppressed the threat by 2014, reducing attacks to near zero. However, incidents have risen since 2023 as Houthi attacks in the Bab al-Mandeb Strait and now the Iran war have drawn naval assets away from anti-piracy patrols. Yemen's Coast Guard acknowledged that its response capabilities remain limited due to the country's dire economic situation.
